name: Recursive Descent
summary: Hand-written parser style: one host-language function per grammar production. Dominant choice for production compilers that need contextual lookahead, custom recovery, and hot-path tuning beyond what generators provide.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Recursive descent deserves an explicit entry because it is not merely an implementation detail behind Pratt parsing or PEG. In its simplest form, each grammar production becomes a host-language function, and the parser advances through the token stream by calling these functions directly.

Variants form a spectrum: predictive recursive descent chooses alternatives from lookahead tokens; backtracking recursive descent tries alternatives speculatively; resilient recursive descent (the rust-analyzer / Matklad pattern) deliberately consumes a well-formed prefix and emits error nodes instead of failing globally.

sections.title: Grammar mapping
sections.content: Each production typically becomes a parsing function. Expression parsing is delegated to Pratt parsing or precedence climbing so that operator logic does not force excessive grammar layering. The usual pattern in production compilers is: parse declarations and statements with recursive descent, parse expressions with Pratt or precedence climbing, and reserve ad-hoc lookahead for the few places where the grammar is intentionally not LL(1).

sections.title: Strengths and production usage
sections.content:
The production appeal is control. A hand-written recursive-descent parser can special-case ambiguous constructs, attach targeted diagnostics, perform context-sensitive recovery, avoid table indirection, and keep hot paths allocation-free.

Status (as of 2026-04): production compilers and toolchains using hand-written recursive descent — typically combined with Pratt or precedence climbing for expressions — include GCC, Clang, Go, rustc, V8, Zig, Ruff, and rust-analyzer. The usual reasons are custom error recovery, contextual lookahead, incremental reparsing, and hot-path tuning that outweigh the convenience of grammar-driven generation.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content:
The cost is that ambiguity detection moves from generator to author. An LR generator will report a shift/reduce conflict; a recursive-descent parser may silently prefer whichever branch its code checks first. Good recursive-descent implementations compensate with tests, explicit grammar comments, debug traces, and carefully named helper functions such as `at_item_start`, `recover_until`, and `expect_contextual_keyword`.

Left recursion typically requires explicit handling, predictive parsing constraints, and grammar refactoring. In larger systems, grammar maintenance may become more ad hoc than in strongly grammar-driven parser-generator workflows.
